markdown:
1. When markets operate **competitively**, the **demand** and **supply** determine the equilibrium price and quantity. 
2. This system in which the **interactions** of consumers and producers determine the prices through **forces** of **demand** and **supply** is referred to as the **price mechanism**.

The **price mechanism** is responsible for two functions in an economy:

1. **Resource allocation: **assigning the factors of production to specific economic activities to efficiently produce goods and services.
2. **Rationing**: the controlled distribution of the scarce resources, goods, and services. 

## Resource allocation


<definition term="Resource allocation">Assigning available resources or factors of production to particular uses selected from various possible options.</definition>

The** price mechanism **affects the** resource allocation** within industries and countries:

1. Producers **sell only** the goods (or services) and quantity of the goods that consumers are **willing and able to buy.**
2. Consumers **buy only** the goods (or services) and quantity of the goods that the producers are **willing and able to supply.**

This **equality** in the buying and selling choices **allocates resources** and determines **what** is being produced and** how much**:

1. Firms will **only use** the resources that consumers are **willing and able** to pay for.
2. Through this mechanism, a free market economy answer the question** what to produce? (and how much)**.

In a nutshell, **prices** function as **signals** and **incentives** that determine the** resource allocation**.

### Signalling function of prices


<definition term="Signalling function of prices">The capacity of **prices**, and **changes** **in** **prices**, to convey **information** to **consumers** and **producers** about the existence of **shortages** or **surpluses** in markets, achieving an **efficient** **allocation** **of** **resources**.</definition>

The **signalling **function of prices communicates important information to buyers and sellers:

1. **Excess demand (shortage)** (too many buyers and not enough goods) leads to **higher** **prices**, **signalling** **producers** **to** **increase** **supply**.
2. **Excess supply (surplus)** (too many goods and not enough buyers) causes **lower prices**, **signalling** **producers** **to** **reduce** **supply**.

<callout type="note">The **signalling **function of prices:

1. Helps consumers and producers make **choices**, guiding **resources** to **where**, and in the **amount** by which, they are **needed** most. 
2. Helps achieve an **efficient use of resources** in the economy.</callout>

### Incentive function of prices


<definition term="Incentive function of prices">The ability of **prices**, and** changes in prices**, to provide **information** to consumers and producers that encourages them to **act in their own** **best** **interest**.</definition>

The **incentive** function of prices guides **consumer** and **producers** by providing them motivation to act in their best self-interest:

1. **Producers** respond to price changes based on the potential for profit, following the **law of supply:**
  1. If the price of a good increases, producers allocate more resources to its production to maximize profits.
  2. Conversely, falling prices may lead to a reduction in supply as producing the good becomes less profitable.
2. **Consumers** react to price changes according to the **law of demand:**
  1. A price drop increases the quantity demanded as the product becomes more affordable.

  * If prices rise, consumers reduce their consumption or seek substitutes.

<callout type="example">1. A rise in the price of smartphones motivates manufacturers to produce and sell more smartphones to capitalize on higher profit margins.
2. A discount on movie tickets incentivizes more people to go to the cinema.</callout>

As producers and consumers are** incentivised** to change their behaviour due to the information** signalled** by prices, this results in a re-allocation of resources.

<callout type="example">![Figure 1: the signalling and incentive function of prices forming a new equilibrium in the market](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9013d73404163fadf97628b705e8c733e3db9e8b-2338x1813.png)

The figure above shows the market for good $X$. **Initially** the market is at equilibrium on point $a$ with price $P_1$ and quantity $Q_1$.

1. Something causes the demand to **increase**, shifting the curve to the **right **(e.g. consumer preferences shift towards good $X$) from $D_1$ to $D_2$
2. This increase in demand causes an excess demand from $Q_2$ to $Q_1$ and thus upward pressure on price drives the prices up till $P_2$.

Hence, now the price acts as a **signal **and **incentive** to both producers and consumers

1. The higher price **signals** to producers that a **shortage** has occurred, and it **incentivizes** them to **increase** their quantity supplied since higher prices are more profitable
2. Hence, the quantity supplied** increases** from $Q_1$ to $Q_3$ for producers
3. However, the higher price also **signals** to consumers that good $X$ is more **expensive** and **incentivizes** them to purchase **less** of the good.
4. Therefore the quantity demanded decreases from $Q_2$ to $Q_3$

Hence, a new equilibrium is reached at point $c$, and resources have been **re-allocated:**

1. A **higher quantity** of good $X$ was being made overall.
2. Now, there is **more allocation of resources** to the production of good $X$.</callout>

## Rationing


<definition term="Rationing">Method to **divide** and **apportion** goods or services amongst consumers</definition>

**Who** gets **how much** of the good or service is completely **determined** by price _(price rationing)_

* People who are **willing and able** to buy the good or service at the price determined by the forces of supply and demand **will get **it.
* People who **are not willing and able to buy it, will not**.

Hence, the **price mechanism** **on its own** determines if a consumer gets a good or service, **dividing the available output** amongst those who are willing and able.

<callout type="example">For instance, if tickets for a popular artist like Chengdu are priced at $\$500, $ only fans with the** highest willingness** (and **ability**) to pay will purchase them.

1. Only those people will get the tickets, **effectively rationing** out the tickets
2. This illustrates how the **rationing function** of the **price mechanism** operates in a market with **scarce **supply (limited tickets).</callout>

<callout type="note">A **very important **thing to note is that this is **only possible** because at the **market-equilibrium price**, the choices of consumers and producers are **matched.**

Therefore those who are willing and able to buy the good (or service) will get it because the firm will be **equally as willing** to supply it at that price.

However, if the market is not free (e.g. _command economy_ or _price controls (2.7)_) then, even if some consumers are willing and able to buy the good or service, they **might not be able to** since producers might not be willing and able to sell it.

In this case an example of rationing would be _first come first serve_ etc. (instead of being determined by the **pricing mechanism**.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">1. How does the price mechanism allocate resources in response to changes in demand or supply?
2. Can you explain the differences between the signalling, incentive, and rationing functions of price?</callout>

<callout type="tok">Is it okay to rely just on the pricing mechanism for allocation and rationing of resources?</callout>

prevtopic.title: 2.3.2 Shifting demand and supply curves to produce a new market equilibrium
